summary: Implementation is solid and spec-compliant across all six modules. Tests are thorough and well-structured. Two issues worth addressing: getRelayerKeySecret is unnecessarily exported from the public index.ts (should stay internal), and OpaqueRelayerKey._extract is a public static method. All other findings are minor.

findings.title: getRelayerKeySecret exported from public npm API surface
findings.description: index.ts re-exports `getRelayerKeySecret` from `relayer-key.ts`, making it part of the public npm package API. The spec explicitly states: 'settle.ts (Task 7) imports getRelayerKeySecret; no other module should' (task spec line 39). Publishing this to the npm surface invites external consumers to call it, which contradicts the access-control intent of D13. settle.ts can import directly from relayer-key.ts without this function appearing in the public barrel.
findings.recommendation: Remove `getRelayerKeySecret` from the `export { ... } from './relayer-key.js'` line in index.ts. settle.ts should import it directly: `import { getRelayerKeySecret } from './relayer-key.js'`.

findings.title: OpaqueRelayerKey._extract is a public static method on the exported class
findings.description: `static _extract(key: OpaqueRelayerKey): string` is defined as a public static method on the exported class. Any code that imports `OpaqueRelayerKey` can call `OpaqueRelayerKey._extract(someKey)` directly, bypassing the brand-check in `getRelayerKeySecret`. The leading underscore is a convention, not enforcement. The actual private-field protection means the key value is still not accessible via property enumeration, but the extraction path is wider than intended. The `_extract` call from `getRelayerKeySecret` is the only internal use, so moving the logic inline into `getRelayerKeySecret` and marking it `#extract` (private method) would be cleaner.
findings.recommendation: Change `static _extract` to a private method `static #extract` and call it from `getRelayerKeySecret` internally. This removes the public bypass path while keeping the same runtime behavior.
findings.spec_reference: task 6 line 39, D13 — 'The ONLY extraction path'

findings.title: PRICE_RE accepts leading zeros (e.g. '00.01', '01')
findings.description: The regex `/^\d+(\.\ d{1,6})?$/` accepts inputs like `'00.01'` and `'01'`. BigInt parsing of `'00'` is valid in JS (equals `0n`), so the math is correct and the result is not wrong. However, the spec's canonical price form for USD amounts would not include leading zeros. This is a permissiveness the task spec doesn't explicitly address, but it could cause upstream price formatting bugs if a caller normalizes `'01.5'` and expects back `1_500_000n` (which it does get correctly). Consider tightening to `^(0|[1-9]\d*)(\.\d{1,6})?$` for clarity.
findings.recommendation: Tighten PRICE_RE to reject leading zeros: `^(0|[1-9]\d*)(\.\d{1,6})?$`. Add test cases for '00.01' and '01' if keeping current behavior. Either way document the behavior explicitly.

findings.title: console.warn at module load for T3 artefact notes not suppressed in test env
findings.description: When `contracts/scripts/arc-testnet-usdc-domain.json` has a non-empty `notes` array (as it does — it includes 'gas estimation fallback applied' and 'arc-dual-decimal' notes), `networks.ts` calls `console.warn` at module load unless `UP_SUPPRESS_T3_NOTES=1` is set. The test suite has no vitest config setting this env var, so every test run that imports `networks.ts` will emit two console.warn lines to stderr. This is not a correctness issue, but it is noisy and may confuse CI log readers.
findings.recommendation: Set `UP_SUPPRESS_T3_NOTES=1` in the vitest config (vitest.config.ts or package.json test env section) for the middleware workspace. Alternatively document this env var in a comment near the `UP_SUPPRESS_T3_NOTES` check so test maintainers know about it.

findings.title: insert() primitive does not call evictExpired before capacity check
findings.description: `has()` calls `#evictExpired` before checking, but `insert()` does not. This means stale TTL entries inflate `#count`, causing premature cap-evictions when callers use the `insert()` primitive directly (e.g. tests using `store.insert(...)` + `store.has(...)`). The primary consumer (`checkAndInsert`) is not affected because it calls `#evictExpired` first. The discrepancy makes the `insert()` primitive behave differently from the `checkAndInsert` path in tests around capacity.
findings.recommendation: Either call `#evictExpired(from, input.now ?? Date.now())` at the start of `insert()` — requiring adding `now` to `InsertInput` — or document the existing behavior explicitly in the `insert()` JSDoc: 'does not evict expired entries; use checkAndInsert for the full pipeline'.

findings.title: Cyclic scrubSecrets test does not verify the cycle node value is scrubbed
findings.description: The cyclic test asserts `out.v` contains REDACTED (the direct value is scrubbed) and `out.self` is defined. However, `scrubSecrets` returns the **original** object reference for already-seen cyclic nodes, not a scrubbed copy. This means `out.self.v` still contains the raw hex string via the original object. The test does not catch this: `out.self === a` (original) and `a.v` is the unscrubbed value. The spec says 'handles cycles without stack-overflow'; it does that. But the redaction guarantee is incomplete for cyclic nodes — documenting this limitation would be helpful.
findings.recommendation: Add a comment to `scrubSecrets` explaining that cyclic back-references return the original un-scrubbed node (a known limitation of non-destructive scrubbing). Optionally add a test assertion: `expect(out.self?.v).not.toContain(RAW_HEX_NO_PREFIX)` — this will currently fail, confirming the limitation exists so it can be explicitly waived.

findings.title: Static class field [BRAND] = true could cause confusion with instance brand stamp
findings.description: The class has `static readonly [BRAND] = true` on the class object itself AND `Object.defineProperty(this, BRAND, { value: true })` on each instance in the constructor. The `is()` method checks `(x)[BRAND] === true` which reads the instance's own property (correct for instances). The static class field is unused by `is()` and serves no purpose other than potentially confusing readers. It also means `OpaqueRelayerKey[BRAND] === true` which could cause a false positive if someone passes the class itself to `is()` — `is(OpaqueRelayerKey)` returns `false` only because it checks `typeof x === 'object'` and the class is `typeof 'function'`. This is fine, but the static field is dead code.
findings.recommendation: Remove `static readonly [BRAND] = true as const` from the class definition — it's unused. The instance brand stamp in the constructor is sufficient for `is()`.

positive_observations: scrubSecrets correctly applies HEX_130 replacement BEFORE HEX_64 to prevent partial matches on 65-byte ECDSA signatures — this ordering is subtle and correct.
NETWORKS uses `as const satisfies Record<string, NetworkConfig>` which provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking while keeping the literal type — good TypeScript practice.
NonceStore checkAndInsert is truly synchronous with no await between has and insert — the TOCTOU contract is met by API shape as the spec requires.
decodeXPayment uses assertExactKeys at every level (top, payload, authorization) — strict shape enforcement prevents future extra-field smuggling.
The arc-mainnet placeholder correctly uses eip155:0 (not eip155:42161) as required by systemic-fix §8 — this guards against Arbitrum One false-positive matches.
networks.ts throws a loud blocker error at module load when T3 artefact is missing — correct fail-fast behavior that prevents silent wrong-domain-hash bugs.
parseUsdPrice uses pure integer arithmetic (no floating point) to avoid IEEE-754 rounding errors in micro-USDC conversion.
The 100k cap eviction in NonceStore is O(n) scan-on-overflow — acceptable given the cap is an absolute ceiling, and the implementation comment explains this trade-off.
